title_en: Prophethood and the Obligations of Faith
content_id:
Bagian besar kedua dari Umm al-Barahin membahas kenabian dan kewajiban-kewajiban spesifik iman Islam. Bagian ini menerapkan kerangka wajib/mustahil/ja'iz kepada para Nabi dan kepada persyaratan-persyaratan khusus beriman kepada Muhammad sebagai Nabi dan Rasul terakhir.

As-Sanusi secara sistematis menetapkan empat sifat wajib para Nabi: sidq (kejujuran), amanah (keterpercayaan), tabligh (penyampaian risalah), dan fatanah (kecerdasan yang sehat). Masing-masing adalah wajib karena misi kenabian memerlukan keempatnya: seorang Nabi yang kejujurannya tidak dapat diandalkan tidak akan mampu berfungsi sebagai pembimbing yang dapat dipercaya; seorang Nabi yang menyembunyikan sebagian risalah akan gagal dalam tugas komunikasi yang esensial; seorang Nabi tanpa pertimbangan yang sehat tidak akan mampu memimpin komunitas dengan bijaksana. Keempat sifat bersama-sama merupakan minimum yang diperlukan agar kenabian menjadi nyata dan efektif.

Berlawanan dengan setiap sifat wajib terdapat kebalikannya yang mustahil: kadhib (dusta), khiyanah (pengkhianatan), kitman (penyembunyian), dan baladah (kedunguan) semuanya niscaya mustahil bagi para Nabi. Ini bukan berarti bahwa para Nabi tidak pernah membuat kesalahan interpretatif atau bahwa penilaian mereka dalam urusan duniawi adalah sempurna — Al-Qur'an sendiri mencatat peristiwa-peristiwa di mana para Nabi dikoreksi oleh Allah dalam hal-hal tertentu. Namun ini berarti bahwa penipuan yang disengaja, pengkhianatan kepercayaan, atau kegagalan kecerdasan yang sistematis dikecualikan oleh sifat kenabian itu sendiri.

Sifat-sifat ja'iz — mungkin — bagi para Nabi mencakup pengalaman manusiawi normal: makan, minum, tidur, menikah, mengalami sakit, dan pada akhirnya meninggal. Pengalaman-pengalaman kontingen ini terjadi dan tercatat dalam Al-Qur'an dan hadis. Keduanya tidak mengurangi martabat atau misi para Nabi tetapi menunjukkan bahwa para manusia kenabian berbagi dalam rangkaian penuh pengalaman manusiawi sambil dibedakan oleh penunjukan ilahi mereka dan sifat-sifat moral dan intelektual mereka yang wajib.

As-Sanusi kemudian membahas kewajiban-kewajiban spesifik setiap Muslim berkenaan dengan pengetahuan tentang Allah dan kenabian. Ia berpendapat bahwa seorang Muslim harus mengetahui sifat-sifat wajib Allah dan sifat-sifat wajib serta hal-hal mustahil berkenaan dengan para Nabi — tidak harus dengan rincian teknis penuh teologi kalam, tetapi setidaknya pada tingkat keyakinan yang tulus. Orang awam (al-awwam) mungkin memiliki pemahaman yang lebih umum dan intuitif tentang perkara-perkara ini, sementara seorang ulama berkewajiban untuk menguasai bukti-bukti demonstratif.

Pembahasan tentang tingkat kewajiban yang berbeda bagi orang-orang awam dan ulama ini adalah salah satu bagian yang secara praktis lebih penting dari Umm al-Barahin. Ia menjawab kekhawatiran yang secara alami muncul dari metodologi kalam karya ini: jika teologi yang benar memerlukan penguasaan bukti-bukti rasional, apakah iman orang-orang awam yang tidak mengetahui bukti-bukti tersebut sah? Jawaban as-Sanusi adalah bahwa keyakinan tulus pada keyakinan-keyakinan yang benar adalah yang terpenting, dan bahwa bukti-bukti rasional berfungsi untuk mengamankan dan membela keyakinan itu daripada menjadi substansi iman itu sendiri.
content_en:
The second major section of Umm al-Barahin addresses prophethood and the specific obligations of Islamic faith. This section applies the wajib/mustahil/ja'iz framework to the Prophets and to the particular requirements of believing in Muhammad as the final Prophet and messenger.

As-Sanusi systematically establishes the four necessary attributes of the Prophets: sidq (truthfulness), amanah (trustworthiness), tabligh (conveyance of the message), and fatanah (sound intelligence). Each of these is necessary because the prophetic mission requires them: a Prophet whose truthfulness could not be relied upon would be unable to serve as a reliable guide; a Prophet who concealed part of the message would fail in the essential task of communication; a Prophet without sound judgment would be unable to lead the community wisely. The four attributes together constitute the minimum necessary for prophethood to be real and effective.

Against each necessary attribute stands its impossible opposite: kadhib (lying), khiyanah (betrayal), kitman (concealment), and baladah (dim-wittedness) are all necessarily impossible for Prophets. This does not mean that Prophets never made interpretive errors or that their judgments in worldly matters were infallible — the Quran itself records instances where Prophets were corrected by God in certain matters. But it does mean that deliberate deception, betrayal of trust, or systematic failure of intelligence are excluded by the nature of prophethood itself.

The ja'iz — possible — attributes of Prophets include normal human experiences: eating, drinking, sleeping, marrying, experiencing illness, and ultimately dying. These contingent experiences occurred and are recorded in the Quran and hadith. They do not compromise the dignity or mission of the Prophets but demonstrate that prophetic human beings shared in the full range of human experience while being distinguished by their divine appointment and their necessary moral and intellectual attributes.

As-Sanusi then addresses the specific obligations on each Muslim regarding the knowledge of God and prophethood. He argues that a Muslim must know the necessary attributes of God and the necessary attributes and impossibilities regarding the Prophets — not necessarily with the full technical detail of kalam theology, but at least at a level of genuine conviction. The common person (al-awwam) may have a more general and intuitive grasp of these matters, while the scholar is obligated to master the demonstrative proofs.

This discussion of the different levels of obligation for ordinary believers and scholars is one of the more practically important sections of Umm al-Barahin. It addresses a concern that arose naturally from the work's kalam methodology: if correct theology requires mastering rational proofs, is the faith of ordinary believers who do not know those proofs valid? As-Sanusi's answer is that genuine conviction in the correct beliefs is what matters, and that rational proofs serve to secure and defend that conviction rather than being themselves the substance of faith.
content_ar: The ثانياً major الفصل of Umm al-Barahin addresses prophethood and the specific obligations of Islamic الإيمان. This الفصل applies the الواجب/mustahil/ja'iz framework to the الأنبياء and to the particular requirements of believing in محمد ﷺ as the final النبي and messenger. As-Sanusi systematically establishes the four necessary attributes of the الأنبياء: sidq (truthfulness), amanah (trustworthiness), tabligh (conveyance of the message), and fatanah (sound intelligence). Each of these is necessary لأن the prophetic mission requires them: a النبي whose truthfulness could not be relied upon would be unable to serve as a reliable guide; a النبي who concealed part of the message would fail in the الجوهري task of communication; a النبي without sound judgment would be unable to lead the الجماعة wisely. The four attributes together constitute the minimum necessary for prophethood to be real and effective. Against each necessary attribute stands its impossible opposite: kadhib (lying), khiyanah (betrayal), kitman (concealment), and baladah (dim-wittedness) are all necessarily impossible for الأنبياء. This does not mean that الأنبياء never made interpretive errors or that their judgments in worldly matters were infallible — the القرآن الكريم itself records instances where الأنبياء were corrected by God in certain matters. But it does mean that deliberate deception, betrayal of trust, or systematic الخسارة of intelligence are excluded by the nature of prophethood itself. The ja'iz — possible — attributes of الأنبياء include normal human experiences: eating, drinking, sleeping, marrying, experiencing المرض, and ultimately dying. These contingent experiences occurred and are recorded in the القرآن الكريم and الحديث. They do not compromise the الكرامة or mission of the الأنبياء but demonstrate that prophetic human beings shared in the full range of human experience بينما being distinguished by their divine appointment and their necessary moral and intellectual attributes. As-Sanusi then addresses the specific obligations on each مسلم فيما يخص the العلم of God and prophethood. He argues that a مسلم must know the necessary attributes of God and the necessary attributes and impossibilities فيما يخص the الأنبياء — not necessarily with the full technical detail of علم الكلام theology, but at least at a level of genuine conviction. The common person (al-awwam) may have a more general and intuitive grasp of these matters, بينما the scholar is obligated to master the demonstrative proofs. This discussion of the different levels of الالتزام for ordinary المؤمنون and العلماء is one of the more practically المهم sections of Umm al-Barahin. It addresses a concern that arose naturally from the work's علم الكلام methodology: إذا correct theology requires mastering rational proofs, is the الإيمان of ordinary المؤمنون who do not know those proofs valid?
